text:
User: superseeded notes that come from the agent can just be dropped from the source of truth once they become superseeded -- supersseded notes from the user should continue to carry throughout the guide -- if something used to work as A and it started to work as B it's ok, even desirable, for the guide to include a nudge to how it used to work (imagine if the refactor of the code didn't completely catch everything and now we have seemingly slightly weird code, but the context of how it used to work before would explain it and provide context (exactly what we want this project to do!) on how things had been implemented in some way

Assistant: Yes — and this is a genuinely important refinement, because it ties the retention rule to the *whole point of the project*. Let me sharpen it.

## The rule you're describing

Supersession isn't one policy — it's **authority-dependent**:

- **Agent-authority claim, superseded → drop** from the source of truth. It was an executor's hypothesis; once replaced it has no lasting value.
- **User-authority claim, superseded → keep, rendered as provenance.** The guide's live tip is the current state (B), but it carries a compact "was A until session N — changed because X" breadcrumb.

And the clean version of "authority" composes with the admission gate you already have: **user-authority = user-authored OR user-ratified.** (A ratified agent claim became user intent, so *its* supersession also deserves to carry as history. An unratified agent claim never entered the spec in the first place, so there's nothing to drop.)

## Why this is the right call, not just a nicety

You named the actual mission: **the wiki exists to explain why the code looks the way it does.** A refactor that went A→B but didn't fully land leaves "weird" code that's *only* explicable if you know it used to be A. Current-tip-only projection throws away exactly the archaeological context that makes proactive-context valuable. So: [...] You named the actual mission: **the wiki exists to explain why the code looks the way it does.** A refactor that went A→B but didn't fully land leaves "weird" code that's *only* explicable if you know it used to be A. Current-tip-only projection throws away exactly the archaeological context that makes proactive-context valuable. So:

> This amends the "desired-state, not events" philosophy: the guide should be **desired-state *plus* the provenance of how user decisions evolved** — not a raw event log, but curated "here's where we are, and here's the load-bearing history of how we got here."

That's a meaningful philosophy refinement, and it's correct.
